reasoning: The Lean statement quantifies over an arbitrary group G with elements a, b satisfying ab = ba, and concludes that every pair of elements in `closure {x | x = a ∨ x = b}` commutes. The set `{x | x = a ∨ x = b}` equals `{a, b}`, so the `closure` (which refers to `Subgroup.closure` via the opened namespace) is exactly the subgroup generated by a and b. Asserting that every two elements of this subgroup commute is equivalent to asserting that the subgroup is abelian, since the group structure on H is inherited from G. The hypothesis and quantifier structure match the informal statement precisely, with no added or dropped assumptions.

The informal statement says: if a group has exactly one element of order 2, that element is in the center. Together these are equivalent to "x is the unique element of order 2". The conclusion `x ∈ center G` matches "that element is in the center of the group". The group G is an arbitrary group (universally quantified as `{G : Type*} [Group G]`), matching the informal universal reading. No hypotheses are silently added or dropped.

The informal statement asserts that every finite group of even order contains an element of order 2. The Lean statement correctly encodes all parts of the informal claim. The hypotheses `Set.Countable S` and `S.Infinite` together mean S is countably infinite, and `span K S = ⊤` says S spans V. `[Field K] [Module K V]` is the standard encoding of "V is a K-vector space." The use of a linearly independent family `R : ι → V` rather than a subset is equivalent: `LinearIndependent K R` forces R to be injective, so ι has the same cardinality as the linearly independent image set, making "every such ι is countable" equivalent to "every linearly independent subset is at most countable."

The informal statement says: if G/Z is cyclic (where Z is the center of G), then G is abelian and G = Z. The Lean signature universally quantifies over finite groups G and primes p, q : ℕ, asserts |G| = p^2*q via card G, and concludes ¬IsSimpleGroup G (encoded as IsSimpleGroup G → False). The informal statement says that in a group, elements $a, b$ generate the same subgroup as $bab^2, bab^3$. No hypotheses are added or dropped and no types are changed.

The informal claim is that in ℤ[x], (2) ∩ (x) = (2x). The Lean signature takes place in `Polynomial ℤ` and uses `span` on singleton sets of type `Set (Polynomial ℤ)`. The informal claim "Z/nZ is not a group under multiplication" for n > 1 is logically equivalent to "not every element of Z/nZ has a two-sided multiplicative inverse with respect to 1". In Mathlib, the definition of `Sylow p H` (via its underlying subgroup) is exactly: being a p-group AND being maximal among p-subgroups. Thus the conjunction `IsPGroup p (P.subgroupOf H) ∧ ∀ Q, IsPGroup p Q → P.subgroupOf H ≤ Q → Q = P.subgroupOf H` is logically equivalent to "P.subgroupOf H ∈ Syl_p(H)". The hypotheses (P : Sylow p G and P ≤ H) also match the informal ones, so the encoding is faithful.

The Lean statement asserts exactly the claim in the informal statement: given a finite group G of order 312, there exists a prime p dividing |G| and a Sylow p-subgroup P of G that is normal. The hypothesis `card G = 312` matches "group of order 312". The existential ∃ p, ∃ P : Sylow p G together with `p.Prime ∧ p ∣ card G ∧ P.Normal` correctly captures "there is a prime p dividing the order such that some Sylow p-subgroup is normal". The `Sylow p G` type in Mathlib gives maximal p-subgroups, which is the correct notion once `p.Prime` is asserted. No extra hypothesis is added and none is dropped, so the formalization is logically equivalent to the informal statement.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement closely. The hypotheses `p < q ∧ q < r`, `p.Prime ∧ q.Prime ∧ r.Prime`, and `card G = p*q*r` together with `[Fintype G]` encode exactly "finite group G with |G| = pqr where p < q < r are primes". The conclusion is a three-way disjunction asserting existence of a normal Sylow p-, q-, or r-subgroup, which is precisely "G has a normal Sylow subgroup for either p, q, or r." The use of `Sylow p G` together with `.Normal` is the standard Mathlib encoding, and no extra hypotheses (like commutativity or solvability) have been added, nor any required ones dropped.

The Lean signature quantifies universally over finite groups G with `card G = 200` and asserts the existence of a Sylow 5-subgroup N with N.Normal. This matches the informal claim "a group of order 200 has a normal Sylow 5-subgroup" exactly: the order hypothesis is encoded by `card G = 200`, and "has a normal Sylow 5-subgroup" is directly rendered as `∃ N : Sylow 5 G, N.Normal`. The `Sylow 5 G` type is Mathlib's standard notion of Sylow 5-subgroup, and `N.Normal` is the standard normality predicate. No extra hypotheses (like solvability, abelian, etc.) are imposed, and no weakening occurs.

The informal statement asserts that any group G of order 1365 is not simple. The Lean theorem universally quantifies over types G with Group and Fintype instances, assumes card G = 1365, and concludes ¬ IsSimpleGroup G. Mathlib's IsSimpleGroup corresponds to the standard notion (nontrivial with only trivial normal subgroups); since |G| = 1365 > 1, the nontriviality condition is automatically consistent. The hypotheses and conclusion match the informal statement exactly.

The informal statement asserts that any group G of order 132 is not simple. The Lean signature takes an arbitrary type G with [Fintype G] and [Group G], assumes card G = 132, and concludes ¬ IsSimpleGroup G. The Fintype assumption is implicit in having finite cardinality 132, so it doesn't add extra content. IsSimpleGroup in Mathlib is the standard notion of simple group (nontrivial with no proper normal subgroups), which matches the informal meaning, and a group of order 132 is automatically nontrivial. The quantifier structure (universal over G) and conclusion match exactly.

The Lean statement captures the informal claim exactly: `card G = 105` matches "order 105", `P : Sylow 3 G` with `[hP : P.Normal]` matches "a Sylow 3-subgroup of G is normal", and `∀ a b : G, a*b = b*a` is the standard definition of G being abelian. The parametric quantification over P in Lean is equivalent to the informal "if a Sylow 3-subgroup is normal" since all Sylow 3-subgroups are conjugate in a finite group (so one being normal is equivalent to the unique one being normal). The Fintype instance is necessary machinery for stating `card G` and Sylow subgroups, not an extra hypothesis beyond the informal claim.

The Lean statement correctly encodes the informal claim. `H.Normal` is the standard normality predicate, and `⁅(⊤ : Subgroup G), H⁆` is Mathlib's commutator bracket between the full subgroup (⊤, which is G viewed as a subgroup of itself) and H, which is exactly the commutator subgroup [G, H] in the informal sense. The biconditional matches the "if and only if" of the informal statement, and there are no extra or missing hypotheses beyond G being a group and H being a subgroup. This is a logically equivalent formalization.

The Lean signature faithfully encodes the informal claim. `[CommRing R] [IsDomain R]` together assert that R is a commutative ring with 1 ≠ 0 and no zero divisors — the standard Dummit & Foote definition of integral domain. The hypothesis `x^2 = 1` matches, and the conclusion `x = 1 ∨ x = -1` is the standard encoding of "x = ±1". No hypotheses are silently added (no finiteness, characteristic, or decidability assumptions) or dropped, and the quantifier structure over the implicit x is preserved.

The Lean statement takes a ring R (via `[Ring R]`) with the hypothesis `hR : ∀ a : R, a^2 = a`, which precisely encodes the Boolean ring condition. The conclusion `∀ a b : R, a*b = b*a` is exactly commutativity of multiplication. The quantifier structure ("every Boolean ring" becomes a universally quantified type variable R with the Boolean hypothesis) is a standard and faithful encoding. No extra hypotheses (like commutativity, finiteness, or characteristic) are added, and none of the informal hypotheses are dropped. The formalization is logically equivalent to the informal claim.

The informal statement asks to show that the sum of all group elements, viewed as an element of the group ring RG, lies in the center of RG, for an arbitrary finite group G and ring R. The Lean version uses `MonoidAlgebra R G` (Mathlib's standard encoding of the group ring), quantifies universally over `R : Type*` with `[Ring R]` and `G : Type*` with `[Group G] [Fintype G]` (matching "finite group"), and expresses N as `∑ g : G, MonoidAlgebra.of R G g`, where `MonoidAlgebra.of` is exactly the canonical embedding of group elements as basis elements of RG. The conclusion `∈ center (MonoidAlgebra R G)` matches "in the center of the group ring". The hypotheses, conclusion, quantifier structure, and types all match the informal statement.

The Lean statement faithfully encodes the informal claim. `ZMod (p^m)` is $\mathbb{Z}/p^m\mathbb{Z}$, and `span ({↑p} : Set (ZMod (p^m)))` is exactly the ideal generated by the image of $p$, i.e., $p\mathbb{Z}/p^m\mathbb{Z}$. Mathlib's `IsNilpotent` on an ideal $I$ states $\exists n, I^n = \bot$, which matches the informal definition (the $n \geq 1$ restriction is automatic since $I^n = 0 \Rightarrow I^{n+1} = 0$). The primality hypothesis is retained (as `_hp`), matching the informal assumption. No hypotheses are silently added or dropped and no types are changed.

The Lean statement captures the content of the informal claim: positive N, integer M with gcd(M,N)=1, exponent d with gcd(d, φ(N))=1, hypothesis M' ≡ M^d (mod N), and conclusion that there exists d' with d'·d ≡ 1 (mod φ(N)) and M ≡ M'^{d'} (mod N). Recasting "the inverse d' mod φ(N)" as an existential ∃d' with d'd ≡ 1 mod φ(N) is logically equivalent, since M'^{d'} mod N only depends on d' mod φ(N). The only minor encoding choice is taking d : ℕ rather than d : ℤ, but since for any integer d with gcd(d, φ(N))=1 one can reduce d mod φ(N) to a nonnegative representative giving the same M^d mod N, the two formulations are equivalent (and in the Dummit–Foote / RSA setting d is naturally a positive integer anyway). No hypothesis is silently added or dropped.

The informal statement asserts: if an integer n is the sum of two rational squares, then it is the sum of two integer squares. The Lean signature quantifies over n : ℤ and r s : ℚ with hypothesis r^2 + s^2 = n (with the natural ℤ → ℚ coercion on n) and concludes ∃ a b : ℤ, a^2 + b^2 = n. Universally quantifying over r, s and concluding with an existential over a, b is logically equivalent to "if there exist rational r, s with r^2 + s^2 = n then there exist integer a, b with a^2 + b^2 = n." The types and quantifier structure match the informal claim exactly.

The informal statement asserts that $\mathbb{Z}[i]/(1+i)$ is a field of order 2. The Lean signature encodes $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ as `GaussianInt`, encodes $1+i$ as `⟨1, 1⟩` (which in `Zsqrtd` is the element with re=1, im=1, i.e. $1+i$), and takes `Ideal.span` of this singleton to form the ideal $(1+i)$. The quotient ring is then formed, and the statement asserts both `IsField` on the quotient (it is a field) and the existence of a `Fintype` instance with `Fintype.card = 2` (it has exactly 2 elements). This precisely captures "is a field of order 2" without adding or removing hypotheses or conclusions.

The Lean statement correctly encodes ℚ[x, y] as `MvPolynomial (Fin 2) ℚ` and the ideal (x, y) as the span of {X 0, X 1}. With `Ideal` opened (and `Submodule` not opened), `span` resolves to `Ideal.span`, producing the ideal generated by the two variables. `Submodule.IsPrincipal` applied to this ideal asserts exactly that the ideal is principal, and the outer negation asserts it is not. The ambient ring, the ideal, and the property negated all align with the informal claim, so this is a logically equivalent formalization.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement: f, g : Polynomial ℚ captures "polynomials with rational coefficients"; the hypothesis `∀ n, ∃ a : ℤ, (f*g).coeff n = a` (with implicit coercion) captures "f*g has integer coefficients"; and the conclusion `∃ a : ℤ, f.coeff i * g.coeff j = a` captures "the product is an integer". The indices i, j appear as free variables in the theorem signature, which in Lean 4 amounts to universal quantification over all natural numbers, matching "any coefficient ... any coefficient". The quantifier structure, ambient types, and hypothesis/conclusion all align with the informal claim.

The informal statement asks to prove that $x^6+30x^5-15x^3+6x-120$ is irreducible in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$. The Lean signature asserts `Irreducible (X^6 + 30*X^5 - 15*X^3 + 6*X - 120 : Polynomial ℤ)`, which is exactly the same polynomial in the same ring ℤ[x], with the same conclusion of irreducibility. The coefficients (1, 30, 0, -15, 0, 6, -120) and degrees match the informal polynomial precisely, and `Irreducible` in `Polynomial ℤ` is the standard notion of irreducibility in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$. No hypotheses or conclusions have been added or dropped.

The Lean encoding correctly captures $\frac{(X+2)^p - 2^p}{X}$: with $f = (X+2)^p$, the coefficients $\binom{p}{n} 2^{p-n}$ are all nonzero, so $f.\text{support} \setminus \{0\} = \{1,\ldots,p\}$, and the sum $\sum_{n=1}^{p} f.\text{coeff}(n) \cdot X^{n-1}$ equals $\sum_{n=1}^{p} \binom{p}{n} 2^{p-n} X^{n-1} = ((X+2)^p - 2^p)/X$. The hypothesis `p.Prime ∧ p > 2` for $p : \mathbb{N}$ is equivalent to "$p$ is an odd prime". `Irreducible` over `Polynomial ℤ` matches irreducibility in $\mathbb{Z}[X]$ (and since the polynomial has degree $p-1 \ge 2$, this coincides with the standard notion of irreducible polynomial). The quantifier structure and conclusion are as stated.

The informal statement asks to prove that $x^2+y^2-1$ is irreducible in $\mathbb{Q}[x,y]$. The Lean signature encodes $\mathbb{Q}[x,y]$ as `MvPolynomial (Fin 2) ℚ`, which is the standard Mathlib representation of the polynomial ring in two variables over $\mathbb{Q}$. The polynomial $(MvPolynomial.X 0)^2 + (MvPolynomial.X 1)^2 - 1$ corresponds to $x^2+y^2-1$, and `Irreducible` is the standard Mathlib definition of irreducibility (not a unit, and not a product of two non-units), which matches the informal notion. There are no extra or missing hypotheses.

The informal statement asserts that any group of order 5 must be abelian. The Lean signature takes an arbitrary type G with a Group instance and Fintype instance, assumes card G = 5, and concludes ∀ a b : G, a*b = b*a, which is precisely the definition of being abelian (commutative). The universal quantification over groups is captured by G being a type variable. No extra hypotheses are added, and no hypotheses are dropped. This is a faithful encoding.

The informal statement asserts that for any finite group G, there exists a positive integer m with a^m = e for all a ∈ G. The Lean signature captures exactly this: `[Group G] [Fintype G]` encodes "finite group", `∃ (m : ℕ), m > 0` encodes "there exists a positive integer m" (ℕ with m > 0 is equivalent to a positive integer for exponentiation purposes), and `∀ (a : G), a ^ m = 1` encodes "a^m = e for all a ∈ G" (1 is the identity in multiplicative group notation). The quantifier structure (∃m, ∀a) matches, and no extra hypotheses (like commutativity) are assumed nor any required hypotheses dropped.

The informal statement posits a group G with the two identities (ab)^3 = a^3 b^3 and (ab)^5 = a^5 b^5 holding for all a, b, and concludes that G is abelian. The Lean signature takes an arbitrary type G with a Group instance, hypothesizes ∀ a b, (a*b)^3 = a^3*b^3 ∧ (a*b)^5 = a^5*b^5 (logically equivalent to two separate ∀ statements for the two identities), and concludes ∀ a b, a*b = b*a, which is precisely the definition of G being abelian. No extra hypotheses (e.g., finiteness, commutativity) are added, and no hypotheses are dropped. The quantifier structure and types match exactly.

The Lean statement faithfully formalizes the informal claim. `centralizer {x⁻¹ * a * x}` is the centralizer of the single element $x^{-1}ax$ (matching $C(x^{-1}ax)$), and `(fun g => x⁻¹ * g * x) '' (centralizer {a})` is precisely $\{x^{-1}gx : g \in C(a)\}$, which is the standard interpretation of $x^{-1}C(a)x$. The universal quantification over groups $G$ and elements $a, x$ is handled by the implicit type variable and explicit arguments. Both sides are sets (via `Set.centralizer` and `Set.image`), making the equality type-correct and semantically equivalent to the informal claim.

The informal statement says "if a > 1 is an integer, then n ∣ φ(a^n − 1)". The Lean signature takes a, n : ℕ with a > 1 and concludes n ∣ (a^n - 1).totient. While the informal says "integer", the constraint a > 1 makes a a positive integer, so encoding as ℕ is equivalent. Mathlib's Nat.totient corresponds to the Euler φ-function, and Nat.sub on a^n - 1 with a ≥ 2 behaves correctly. The case n = 0 works trivially (both sides become 0 ∣ 0, which holds). No hypotheses are added or dropped relative to the informal claim, so the formalization is faithful.

The Lean statement captures all the hypotheses of the informal statement: G is a finite group with `card G = p*m`, p is prime (`Nat.Prime p`), p does not divide m (`¬ p ∣ m`), and H is a normal subgroup of G (`[H.Normal]`) with order p (`card H = p`). The conclusion `Subgroup.Characteristic H` matches the informal conclusion "H is characteristic". The finiteness of G is implicit in the informal statement (since $|G|=pm$ is a natural number product) and is correctly encoded as `[Fintype G]`. All quantifier structure, types, and mathematical notions align, so this is a faithful formalization.

The informal statement has three components: (1) G is a group of order 6, (2) G is nonabelian, (3) G ≅ S_3. The Lean signature captures each faithfully: `[Group G] [Fintype G]` with `card G = 6` expresses "finite group of order 6"; `∃ a b : G, a * b ≠ b * a` is exactly the negation of commutativity, i.e. nonabelian; and `Nonempty (G ≃* Equiv.Perm (Fin 3))` expresses that there exists a group isomorphism between G and the symmetric group on 3 elements (S_3 = Sym({1,2,3}) = Perm (Fin 3)). The quantifier structure, types, and hypotheses all match the informal claim, so this is a strictly faithful formalization.

The informal statement asserts that any group of order p² (p prime) has a normal subgroup of order p. The Lean signature takes a finite group G with `card G = p^2` and `p` prime, and concludes the existence of a subgroup N with `card N = p` that is normal. The existentially introduced `Fintype N` instance is purely a technical scaffold for stating `card N = p` (any subgroup of a finite group is finite), and doesn't change the mathematical content. The hypotheses and conclusion align exactly with the informal claim.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement component by component: `[CommGroup G]` captures "abelian group"; the two existential hypotheses `∃ g, orderOf g = m` and `∃ g, orderOf g = n` capture "has an element of order m" and "of order n"; `m.Coprime n` captures "relatively prime"; and the conclusion `∃ g, orderOf g = m * n` matches "has an element of order mn". Types and quantifier structure align, and edge cases around m or n being 0 are handled consistently by Mathlib's `orderOf` convention together with `Nat.Coprime`. No hypotheses have been silently added or dropped.

The Lean statement takes two finite groups G and H with `card G = 21` and `card H = 21`, both assumed nonabelian via `∃ a b, a * b ≠ b * a` (the standard negation of the abelian condition), and concludes `Nonempty (G ≃* H)`, i.e. they are isomorphic as groups. This matches the informal claim that "any two nonabelian groups of order 21 are isomorphic" exactly: the universal quantification is captured by the arbitrary type variables G, H, the hypotheses encode "nonabelian of order 21", and the conclusion encodes "isomorphic". The `Fintype` instance is needed to make `card` well-defined and is implied by "order 21" in the informal statement. No hypothesis is added or dropped, and no condition is strengthened or weakened.

The informal statement describes two cyclic groups of orders m and n (implicitly finite since they have finite orders) and claims G₁ × G₂ is cyclic iff m and n are coprime. The Lean version uses `Fintype G`, `Fintype H`, `Group G`, `Group H`, `IsCyclic G`, `IsCyclic H`, making the groups finite cyclic groups. The `card G` and `card H` correspond to the orders m and n (since for a finite group, `Fintype.card` equals the group's order). The conclusion `IsCyclic (G × H) ↔ (card G).Coprime (card H)` matches the informal iff. Quantifier structure is preserved via the universe-polymorphic `{G H : Type*}`. This is a faithful encoding.

The Lean statement faithfully captures the informal claim: P is a Sylow p-subgroup (`P : Sylow p G`), p is prime (`hp : Nat.Prime p`), P is normal in G (`hP : P.Normal`), and the conclusion that P is the unique Sylow p-subgroup is expressed as `∀ (Q : Sylow p G), P = Q`. Equality of `Sylow p G` terms in Mathlib corresponds to equality as subgroups, so this correctly states uniqueness. No extra hypotheses (like finiteness) are assumed that would not already be implicit in the informal statement, and no hypotheses are dropped.

The Lean signature captures exactly the informal claim: for a prime p, a finite group G of order p^n, and a subgroup K of order p^{n-1}, K is normal. The prime hypothesis is made explicit (standard reading of "order p^n"), the cardinality conditions match, and the conclusion `K.Normal` matches "normal in G". Universal quantification over K is encoded correctly via hypothesis binding. The redundant `[Fintype K]` is derivable from G finite and does not add or remove content. Edge cases (n = 0 with Nat truncation) resolve consistently to a trivial subgroup, which is normal.

The informal statement asks to show there are infinitely many solutions to x² = -1 in the quaternions. The Lean statement asserts that the set {x : Quaternion ℝ | x^2 = -1} is Infinite. Quaternion ℝ is the standard real quaternions (Hamilton's quaternions), the set-builder captures exactly the solutions of x² = -1, and `Infinite` is Mathlib's predicate for having infinitely many elements. The quantifier, ambient type, and conclusion all match what the informal statement requires.

The informal statement assumes R is a ring satisfying x^3 = x for all x ∈ R, and concludes that R is commutative. The Lean signature takes R with the `[Ring R]` typeclass (matching "ring"), uses the hypothesis `∀ x : R, x ^ 3 = x` (exactly the informal hypothesis), and concludes `∀ x y : R, x * y = y * x`, which is precisely the definition of commutativity of multiplication in a ring. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the type and quantifier structure match. This is a faithful formalization.

The informal statement says: for odd prime p, if the harmonic sum 1 + 1/2 + ... + 1/(p-1) equals a/b with a, b integers, then p divides a. The Lean statement quantifies over all integers a, b such that (a/b : ℚ) equals the sum, and concludes p ∣ a. Since every integer representation a/b of a rational H = A/B (in lowest terms) has a = kA for some integer k, the universal claim "p ∣ a for all such a" is equivalent to "p ∣ A" (the numerator in lowest terms), which is the standard meaning of the informal statement. The hypotheses (Nat.Prime p, Odd p) correctly capture "odd prime", the sum Finset.range (p-1) with term 1/(i+1) correctly gives 1 + 1/2 + ... + 1/(p-1), and edge cases like b = 0 are handled vacuously since the harmonic sum is nonzero. The encoding is logically equivalent to the informal claim.

The Lean statement uses `TwoSidedIdeal` for the 2×2 real matrix ring, which matches the standard non-commutative ring theory reading of "ideal" in Herstein's textbook (the result is the simplicity of M_2(ℝ), a theorem about two-sided ideals; one-sided ideals of M_2(ℝ) are plentiful). The ring `Matrix (Fin 2) (Fin 2) ℝ` correctly encodes 2×2 matrices over ℝ, and `⊥`/`⊤` correspond to the zero ideal and the whole ring. The conclusion `I = ⊥ ∨ I = ⊤` is the faithful translation of "I = (0) or I = R".

The Lean statement captures exactly the informal claim. The hypotheses match: `p` is prime (`hp : Nat.Prime p`), `q` is a polynomial over `ZMod p` (which is the field $\mathbb{Z}_p$ since `p` is prime), `q` is irreducible, and `q.degree = n` (the `WithBot ℕ` coercion is unambiguous since irreducible polynomials are nonzero). The conclusion has the two parts asked for: (i) the quotient `Polynomial (ZMod p) ⧸ span {q}` is finite with cardinality $p^n$ (via the existentially quantified `Fintype` instance and `Fintype.card = p^n`), and (ii) it is a field (`IsField`). Since all `Fintype` instances on a type give the same cardinality, the existential is equivalent to the quotient being finite of size $p^n$. The ideal `span {q}` here is `Ideal.span {q}` (the namespace is opened), which is the principal ideal generated by `q`, matching `(q(x))`. No extra hypotheses are assumed and none are dropped.

The Lean statement precisely captures the informal claim. The hypothesis `Nat.Prime p` corresponds to "p is prime". The sum `∑ i ∈ Finset.range p, X ^ i` expands to `X^0 + X^1 + ... + X^(p-1) = 1 + X + X^2 + ... + X^(p-1)`, which is exactly the polynomial q(x) in the informal statement. The ambient ring `Polynomial ℚ` matches $\mathbb{Q}[x]$, and `Irreducible` in a polynomial ring over a field is the standard notion of irreducibility. The statement is universally quantified over primes p, matching the informal quantifier structure.

The Lean statement defines the set of integers `a : ℤ` for which the polynomial `X^7 + 15X^2 - 30X + a` in `Polynomial ℚ` is irreducible, and asserts this set is infinite. This matches the informal claim exactly: the parameter `a` ranges over integers, the polynomial is considered in ℚ[x] (via the coercion to `Polynomial ℚ`), irreducibility is the standard Mathlib notion, and `Infinite` on the set captures "infinite number". No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the types (ℤ for the parameter, Polynomial ℚ for the polynomial) align with the informal statement.

The Lean signature faithfully captures the informal claim. `[Infinite F] [Field F]` encodes "infinite field", `[AddCommGroup V] [Module F V]` encodes "vector space over F", `[Finite ι]` with an indexed family `u : ι → Submodule F V` encodes "a finite number of subspaces", and `hu : ∀ i, u i ≠ ⊤` correctly states that each subspace is proper (since ⊤ is the whole space). The conclusion `(⋃ i, (u i : Set V)) ≠ ⊤` expresses that the set-theoretic union of these subspaces is not all of V (⊤ in Set V is univ). Edge cases such as empty ι or trivial V are handled consistently with the informal reading. No hypotheses are silently added or dropped.

The informal statement asks to prove that cos 1° is algebraic over ℚ. Since 1° equals π/180 radians, cos 1° = cos(π/180). The Lean statement `IsAlgebraic ℚ (cos (Real.pi / 180))` asserts exactly that cos(π/180) is algebraic over ℚ. The conversion from degrees to radians is correct, and `IsAlgebraic ℚ` is the standard Mathlib notion of being algebraic over ℚ. This is a faithful formalization.

The informal statement asks to prove that the polynomial $x^3 - 3x - 1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$. The Lean statement asserts `Irreducible (X^3 - 3*X - 1 : Polynomial ℚ)`, which directly encodes the same polynomial in $\mathbb{Q}[X]$ and uses Mathlib's `Irreducible` predicate. The polynomial expression, the ring, and the conclusion all match exactly, so this is a strictly faithful formalization.

The Lean statement captures the informal claim correctly. The universal quantification over odd naturals matches the "for all odd n" in the informal statement. Since n is odd, n ≥ 1, so truncated subtraction n^2 - 1 in ℕ coincides with standard subtraction and poses no issue. Even under the interpretation that the informal statement quantifies over all odd integers, the ℕ version is logically equivalent because (-n)^2 = n^2, so divisibility of n^2 - 1 by 8 for positive odd n immediately yields the same conclusion for negative odd n. The conclusion 8 ∣ (n^2 - 1) matches the informal statement exactly.

The informal statement asks to show that $(1+i)^2$ divides $2$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$. In Mathlib, `GaussianInt` is exactly $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (defined as `Zsqrtd (-1)`), and `⟨1, 1⟩ : GaussianInt` is the element with real part 1 and imaginary part 1, i.e., $1+i$. The Lean expression `(⟨1, 1⟩ : GaussianInt) ^ 2 ∣ 2` reads "$(1+i)^2$ divides $2$", which is precisely the informal claim "$2$ is divisible by $(1+i)^2$". The ambient ring, the element $1+i$, the squaring, and the divisibility relation all match exactly.

The hypotheses `hl` and `hl1` together uniquely determine `l` to be the von Mangoldt function Λ: `hl` pins down the value log p on every prime power p^n (n ≥ 1), and `hl1` forces the value 0 on every non-prime-power (including 0 and 1, since Mathlib's `IsPrimePow` requires the exponent to be positive). By unique factorization, p^n with n ≥ 1 uniquely determines the prime p, so `hl` is well-defined. The conclusion states the function equality l = (n ↦ ∑_{d ∣ n} μ(n/d) log d), which is exactly the identity Λ(n) = ∑_{d ∣ n} μ(n/d) log d claimed informally (for n = 0 both sides are 0 since `Nat.divisors 0 = ∅`). No extra hypotheses are added and none are dropped, and the quantifier structure matches.

The Lean statement `Infinite {p : Nat.Primes // p ≡ -1 [ZMOD 6]}` expresses exactly the informal claim: the subtype consists of primes p (via `Nat.Primes`) such that p ≡ -1 (mod 6) (via `Int.ModEq 6`), and `Infinite` asserts that this collection is infinite. The notation `[ZMOD 6]` gives integer congruence modulo 6 (equivalently p ≡ 5 mod 6), which is what "congruent to -1 modulo 6" means. No extra hypotheses are added and no hypothesis is dropped; quantifier structure and conclusion match the informal statement.

The informal statement asserts that the equation 7x³ + 2 = y³ has no integer solutions. The Lean statement `¬ ∃ x y : ℤ, 7*x^3 + 2 = y^3` directly expresses that there do not exist integers x and y satisfying this equation. The types (ℤ), the equation, and the quantifier structure (negation of existence) all match the informal claim exactly. This is a straightforward and strictly faithful formalization.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement component by component. "Odd primes" is encoded as `p.Prime ∧ p > 2` and `q.Prime ∧ q > 2`, which is equivalent since 2 is the only even prime. "Distinct" becomes `p ≠ q`. "$p-1$ divides $q-1$" becomes `p - 1 ∣ q - 1`; natural-number truncated subtraction is fine here because `p, q > 2` ensures `p, q ≥ 3`. The coprimality `(n, pq) = 1` is encoded as `n.gcd (p*q) = 1`, and the conclusion `n^{q-1} ≡ 1 (pq)` is `n^(q-1) ≡ 1 [MOD p*q]`. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the quantifier structure is preserved.

The informal statement says: for a prime p ≡ 3 (mod 4) and a unit a modulo p, a is a primitive root mod p iff −a has order (p−1)/2. The Lean signature takes p prime with p = 4t+3 (which is exactly p ≡ 3 mod 4) and a ∈ (ZMod p)ˣ, and states IsPrimitiveRoot a (p−1) ↔ IsPrimitiveRoot (−a) ((p−1)/2). `IsPrimitiveRoot x n` in Mathlib (for n ≥ 1) is logically equivalent to "x has order n" — it asserts x^n = 1 and that n divides every l with x^l = 1. So the left side says a has order p−1 (i.e., is a primitive root), and the right side says −a has order (p−1)/2. The universal quantification over a is captured by a being a free variable. There is no added or dropped hypothesis, no type mismatch, and the iff structure matches.

The Lean signature captures exactly the informal claim. `Odd p` together with `p.Prime` encodes "odd prime". Taking `a : (ZMod p)ˣ` is equivalent to a being coprime to p, which is the natural domain for the primitive root notion. `IsPrimitiveRoot (a : ZMod p) (p - 1)` is Mathlib's standard encoding of "a has order p-1 in (ZMod p)ˣ", i.e., a is a primitive root mod p. The RHS `∀ q, q ∣ (p-1) → q.Prime → ¬ a^((p-1)/q) = 1` is exactly "for every prime divisor q of p-1, a^((p-1)/q) ≠ 1 in ZMod p". The iff, quantifier structure, and types all match the informal statement.

The Lean statement captures the universal quantification over both x : ℤ and prime p : ℕ via implicit arguments with hypotheses, matching the informal "any prime divisor" reading. The hypothesis (p : ℤ) ∣ (x^4 - x^2 + 1) exactly encodes "p is a prime divisor of x^4 - x^2 + 1", and the conclusion (p : ℤ) ≡ 1 [ZMOD 12] correctly expresses "p ≡ 1 mod 12". Types (p : ℕ, x : ℤ) are standard and no extraneous hypotheses (e.g., p > 3) are added or dropped. The statement is logically equivalent to the informal claim.

The Lean statement faithfully captures the Ireland-Rosen exercise: `a : ℤ` with `ha : a < 0` matches "a is negative"; `p ≡ q [ZMOD 4*a]` matches `p ≡ q (4a)` (Int.ModEq is defined via divisibility and is sign-insensitive, so negative modulus is fine); `¬ ((p : ℤ) ∣ a)` matches `p ∤ a`; and `legendreSym p a = legendreSym q a` matches `(a/p) = (a/q)`. The informal statement doesn't explicitly require `q ∤ a`, but this is implied by the hypotheses (if `q | a`, then `q | p - q | p`, forcing `q = p`, contradicting `p ∤ a`). The `p = 2` edge case is vacuously handled: `2 ∤ a` forces `a` odd, which combined with `2 ≡ q (mod 4a)` forces `q = 2 = p`.

The informal statement requires two representations n = a³+b³ = c³+d³ where in each representation the two cubes are different ("sum of two different integral cubes"). The Lean predicate enforces x ≠ z ∧ x ≠ w ∧ y ≠ z ∧ y ≠ w (pair disjointness) but does NOT require x ≠ y or z ≠ w within each pair. Thus Lean's predicate Q is strictly weaker than the informal P, making the set in which 1729 must be the infimum strictly larger. Consequently, Lean's claim "sInf{Q} = 1729" implies the informal claim (since P ⊆ Q and 1729 ∈ P) but is not implied by it—proving the Lean claim requires additionally ruling out any smaller n expressible as 2a³ = c³+d³ with c ≠ d, c,d ≠ a. The Lean statement is therefore logically stronger than the informal.

The Lean signature captures the informal statement exactly. The ambient setup (topological space X, subset A) matches. The hypothesis `∀ x ∈ A, ∃ U : Set X, x ∈ U ∧ IsOpen U ∧ U ⊆ A` directly encodes "for each x ∈ A there is an open set U containing x such that U ⊆ A", with the correct quantifier order and all three conjuncts (x ∈ U, U open, U ⊆ A). The conclusion `IsOpen A` matches "A is open in X". No extra hypotheses (e.g., no Fintype, T2, etc.) are assumed, and none are dropped.

The Lean signature faithfully captures the informal claim: it takes an arbitrary index type `I` and a family `T : I → Set (Set X)` with each `T i` satisfying `is_topology`, and concludes `⋂ i, T i` satisfies `is_topology`. The `is_topology` predicate encodes the three standard topology axioms (univ membership, closed under binary intersection, closed under arbitrary unions), from which `∅ ∈ T` follows via `sUnion ∅`. The quantifier structure ("for any family") is preserved via universal quantification over `I` and `T`, and no extra hypotheses (like Nonempty, Fintype, or decidability) are imposed. The types match — `Set (Set X)` for a topology on `X`, and the indexed intersection `⋂ i : I, T i` correctly represents $\bigcap \mathcal{T}_\alpha$.

The Lean statement asserts existence and uniqueness of a topology T' on X that (i) is a topology, (ii) contains each T_i, and (iii) is contained in every topology containing all T_i. Conditions (ii) and (iii) together are exactly the universal property of the "smallest topology containing all T_α" in the informal statement, and ∃! captures the uniqueness claim. The family is correctly modeled as an indexed family T : I → Set (Set X) with the hypothesis that each T i is a topology, matching "family of topologies on X". No extra or missing hypotheses are introduced, so the formalization is logically equivalent to the informal claim.

The informal claim asserts that the topology generated by A equals the intersection of all topologies on X that contain A. The Lean LHS `generateFrom A` is exactly "the topology generated by A". The Lean RHS is `generateFrom S` where S = `sInter {T | is_topology X T ∧ A ⊆ T}` is the intersection of all topologies containing A. Since S is itself a topology (intersection of topologies is a topology), applying `generateFrom` to S yields the TopologicalSpace whose opens are exactly S — that is, `GenerateOpen S = (· ∈ S)`. So the Lean equality `generateFrom A = generateFrom S` is equivalent to `opens(generateFrom A) = S`, which is precisely the informal claim. The extra `generateFrom` on the RHS is a harmless coercion from `Set (Set X)` to `TopologicalSpace X` and does not change the mathematical content. The `IsTopologicalBasis` hypothesis matches "A is a basis for a topology on X".

The informal statement asserts that the lower limit topology ℝ_l and the K-topology ℝ_K are not comparable, which means neither topology is finer than the other. The Lean statement is the conjunction of ¬(∀ U, Rl.IsOpen U → K_topology.IsOpen U) and ¬(∀ U, K_topology.IsOpen U → Rl.IsOpen U), which precisely captures that neither topology contains the other's open sets. The auxiliary definitions match Munkres: `lower_limit_topology` is generated by half-open intervals [a,b), `K = {1/(n+1) : n ∈ ℕ} = {1/m : m ∈ ℤ₊}` matches Munkres' K, and `K_topology` is generated by open intervals together with sets of the form (a,b)\K. The quantifier structure and the logical form of "not comparable" are encoded correctly.

The informal statement, in the Munkres sense, asserts two things: (1) T satisfies the basis axioms, and (2) the topology generated by T is different from the lower limit topology. The Lean `IsTopologicalBasis T` in Mathlib is defined with respect to the ambient topology and has three conditions, the last being `eq_generateFrom : ‹TopologicalSpace α› = generateFrom s`. Since ℝ carries its default (standard Euclidean) topology, the Lean statement additionally asserts that the standard topology on ℝ equals `generateFrom T` — an extra claim beyond just "T is a basis". Thus the Lean statement logically implies the informal one (stripping the extra claim leaves the basis axioms) but adds a conclusion (T generates the Euclidean topology on ℝ) not present in the informal text, making it strictly stronger than what was asked.

The informal statement asks to prove that the projections π₁ and π₂ from X × Y are open maps. The Lean signature introduces π₁ and π₂ as arbitrary functions with the hypotheses h₁ : π₁ = Prod.fst and h₂ : π₂ = Prod.snd, and concludes IsOpenMap π₁ ∧ IsOpenMap π₂. Although this is a slightly indirect encoding, under those equality hypotheses the conclusion is logically equivalent to IsOpenMap Prod.fst ∧ IsOpenMap Prod.snd, which is exactly the informal claim. The quantification over arbitrary topological spaces X and Y is also preserved. No extra hypotheses are imposed and none are dropped, so this is a faithful formalization.

The Lean statement captures exactly the informal claim: given an arbitrary topological space X with U open and A closed, it asserts that U \ A is open and A \ U is closed. The set difference `\` in Lean corresponds precisely to the "minus" operation in the informal statement, and the hypotheses `IsOpen U` and `IsClosed A` match the informal hypotheses. No extra assumptions (e.g., finiteness, separation axioms) are introduced, and the conjunction matches both conclusions asked. This is logically equivalent to the informal claim.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement: `LinearOrder Y` corresponds to Munkres' "ordered set" (simple/total order), `OrderTopology Y` asserts that Y's topology is the order topology, and `min` in a `LinearOrder` is exactly the pointwise minimum of the informal h(x) = min{f(x), g(x)}. The hypotheses (continuity of f and g) and the conclusion (continuity of the min) correspond one-to-one with the informal claim, with universal quantification over X, Y, f, g correctly expressed via type variables and typeclass assumptions.

The informal claim is the classical characterization of convergence in the product topology: a sequence in ∏ X_α converges to x iff each projection converges. The Lean statement takes an arbitrary index type ι, a family f : ι → Type* with topological space instances on each factor, a sequence x : ℕ → Π a, f a, and a point y in the product. Mathlib's default topology on Π a, f a is the product topology via `Pi.topologicalSpace`, so `Tendsto x atTop (𝓝 y)` means convergence in the product topology. The right side `∀ i, Tendsto (λ j => (x j) i) atTop (𝓝 (y i))` expresses exactly coordinatewise convergence of the projections π_i(x_j) to π_i(y). Quantifier structure, hypotheses, and conclusion match faithfully.

The Lean statement faithfully captures the informal claim. The domain `I = Icc 0 1` matches $[0,1]$, the hypothesis `h` binds `f n x = x^n`, and the conclusion `∀ x, ∃ y, Tendsto (λ n => f n x) atTop (𝓝 y)` is the standard formalization of "the sequence $(f_n(x))$ converges for each $x \in [0,1]$". The quantifier over `x : I` correctly ranges over $[0,1]$, and the existence of a limit `y` is exactly what convergence means. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the types match.

The Lean signature directly mirrors the informal statement: X is a topological space, Y is a metric space, `f : ℕ → X → Y` with `∀ n, Continuous (f n)` encodes the sequence of continuous functions, `Tendsto x atTop (𝓝 x₀)` encodes x_n → x, `TendstoUniformly f f₀ atTop` encodes uniform convergence of f_n to f, and `Tendsto (fun n => f n (x n)) atTop (𝓝 (f₀ x₀))` encodes the conclusion f_n(x_n) → f(x). No extra hypotheses are added (e.g., no completeness or Nonempty assumption on Y), and none are dropped. The quantifier structure and types match exactly.

The Lean statement captures exactly the informal claim. X is any topological space, A : Set X gets the subspace topology (as a subtype) which is the natural reading of "A ⊂ X". The map r : X → A being Continuous with r x = x for all x : A encodes "continuous retraction r: X → A with r(a) = a for a ∈ A" (equality in the subtype coincides with equality of the underlying elements). Surjectivity onto A follows automatically from the fixed-point condition. IsQuotientMap r is exactly the conclusion "r is a quotient map" (surjective plus codomain topology coinduced by r). No hidden hypotheses or strengthening/weakening is introduced.

The Lean signature faithfully captures Munkres Exercise 23.2. The sequence `A : ℕ → Set X` encodes "a sequence of subspaces," `IsConnected (A n)` encodes "connected" (Mathlib's `IsConnected` is nonempty + preconnected, but the hypothesis `A n ∩ A (n+1) ≠ ∅` already forces each `A n` to be nonempty, so this is equivalent to Munkres's notion in this context). The pairwise-consecutive nonempty intersection hypothesis `A n ∩ A (n+1) ≠ ∅` matches exactly. The conclusion `IsConnected (⋃ n, A n)` matches "⋃ Aₙ is connected" — the union is automatically nonempty since the Aₙ are. No hypotheses are added or dropped and no quantifier structure is altered.

The informal statement asks to show that an infinite set X is connected under the finite complement topology. The Lean statement takes `X : Type*` with `[Infinite X]` (matching "infinite set") and uses `CofiniteTopology X` which is exactly Mathlib's finite complement topology. The conclusion `IsConnected (Set.univ : Set (CofiniteTopology X))` asserts that the whole space is connected. Since `IsConnected` requires nonemptiness and preconnectedness, and `Infinite X` gives nonemptiness automatically, `IsConnected univ` is equivalent to `ConnectedSpace (CofiniteTopology X)`, which is the standard reading of "X is connected in this topology." No hypotheses have been added or dropped, and the conclusion matches.

The Lean statement reframes "connected spaces X, Y with proper subsets A, B" as "arbitrary topological spaces X, Y with connected subsets A₂, B₂ and proper subsets A₁ ⊊ A₂, B₁ ⊊ B₂", with conclusion on (A₂ ×ˢ B₂) \ (A₁ ×ˢ B₁). These are logically equivalent: taking A₂ = univ, B₂ = univ recovers the informal statement (since IsConnected univ ↔ ConnectedSpace), and conversely the Lean statement follows from the informal one by applying it to the subspace topologies on A₂ and B₂ (since subspace topology commutes with products). The set-builder {x | ∃ a b, x = (a,b) ∧ a ∈ A₂ ∧ b ∈ B₂} is exactly A₂ ×ˢ B₂, and ⊂ in Mathlib is proper subset. No hypotheses are silently added or dropped.

The Lean encoding represents $S^1$ as the unit sphere in $\mathbb{C}$ (i.e., `Metric.sphere (0 : ℂ) 1`), which is exactly the circle. The function is continuous and real-valued as required. The conclusion $\exists x, f(x) = f(-x)$ uses Mathlib's `Neg` instance on spheres centered at $0$, which coincides with the antipodal map on $S^1$. Domain, codomain, hypothesis, and conclusion all correspond precisely to the informal statement, so the formalization is logically equivalent.

The Lean signature captures exactly the informal claim: X is `LocPathConnectedSpace` (locally path connected), U is an open (`IsOpen U`) connected (`IsConnected U`) subset, and the conclusion is `IsPathConnected U`. The universal quantifier over connected open sets in X is faithfully expressed by taking an arbitrary U with the stated hypotheses. Mathlib's `IsConnected` (nonempty + preconnected) and `IsPathConnected` (nonempty + path-connected via joins) are consistent with each other, so the nonemptiness convention aligns on both sides, matching the standard textbook reading in Munkres. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the types are correct.

The Lean statement matches Munkres 26.11 under the standard mathematical reading. The compact Hausdorff hypothesis matches (`CompactSpace` + `T2Space`). The chain condition `∀ a b ∈ A, a ⊆ b ∨ b ⊆ a` is logically equivalent to "simply ordered by proper inclusion" on a collection of sets (total order by ⊆ iff strict total order by ⊊ on distinct elements). Closed and connected conditions map directly. The one subtle point is that Lean's `IsConnected` means `Nonempty ∧ IsPreconnected`, and Lean adds the explicit `A.Nonempty` hypothesis, whereas Munkres' formal definition of "connected" technically admits the empty set. However, in the standard reading of this exercise (and standard math usage of "connected subset"), the sets are implicitly nonempty and A is implicitly nonempty—otherwise the result is trivial or ill-posed (empty A would force X itself to be connected, which isn't given). So the Lean encoding faithfully captures the intended content of the exercise.

The Lean statement captures each component of the informal claim precisely: `[MetricSpace X]` gives the metric space structure, `[ConnectedSpace X]` gives connectedness, `∃ x y : X, x ≠ y` is the standard encoding of "more than one point" (i.e., at least two distinct points), and `¬ Countable (univ : Set X)` expresses uncountability of X (via the subtype coercion, this is equivalent to `¬ Countable X`). There are no extra hypotheses (no Fintype, Nonempty, etc.) and no weakening of the conclusion. The formalization is logically equivalent to the informal statement.

The Lean `countably_compact` definition—every ℕ-indexed open cover admits a finite subcover—is equivalent to the standard "every countable open cover has a finite subcover" (any countable cover can be enumerated by ℕ with repetition). The RHS of the iff correctly encodes "nested sequence of closed nonempty sets has nonempty intersection": `IsClosed (C n)` captures closed, `C n ≠ ∅` captures nonempty, `C (n+1) ⊆ C n` captures the nesting (indexing from 0 vs 1 is immaterial), and `∃ x, ∀ n, x ∈ C n` captures that the intersection ⋂ₙ Cₙ is nonempty. No hypotheses are silently added or dropped, and both directions of the iff are stated, matching the informal claim exactly.

The informal statement asks to show that ℚ is not locally compact. The Lean signature `¬ LocallyCompactSpace ℚ` directly asserts exactly this: the rationals (with their default topology inherited as a subspace of ℝ in Mathlib) do not form a locally compact space. There are no added or omitted hypotheses, the conclusion matches, and `LocallyCompactSpace` is the standard Mathlib notion of local compactness. This is a direct, logically equivalent formalization.

The Lean signature faithfully encodes Munkres Exercise 29.10. Hausdorff is captured via T2Space. The local compactness at x hypothesis (∃ U open containing x, with U ⊆ K for some compact K) matches Munkres' definition of "locally compact at x" (a compact subspace containing a neighborhood of x). The "neighborhood U of x" hypothesis uses IsOpen U ∧ x ∈ U, which is Munkres' convention for neighborhood. The conclusion requires V open, x ∈ V, closure V compact, and closure V ⊆ U, which matches exactly. No drift in hypotheses, conclusion, quantifier structure, or types.

The Lean hypotheses and conclusion match the informal statement: `h` encodes "X has a countable dense subset," `hU` encodes pairwise disjointness via the standard `x ≠ y → x ∩ y = ∅` formulation, `hUopen` says each member is open, and the conclusion `Countable U` says the collection is countable. The universal quantification over collections U is correctly captured by making U a parameter. `Countable` on a `Set` (whether resolved to `Set.Countable` or the typeclass on the subtype) gives the same standard notion of at-most-countable. No extra or missing hypotheses appear, and the types (topological space X, sets, open sets) match the informal reading.

The Lean statement matches the informal claim component-by-component. `[NormalSpace X]` captures "X is normal"; `IsClosed A`, `IsClosed B`, `Disjoint A B` captures "disjoint closed sets"; the existence of open sets U, V with A ⊆ U and B ⊆ V captures "neighborhoods" (Munkres' convention treats neighborhoods as open sets containing the set, and even under the looser convention this is equivalent by passing to interiors); and `closure U ∩ closure V = ∅` captures "whose closures are disjoint". No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the quantifier structure (∀ disjoint closed pairs, ∃ neighborhoods) is preserved.

The informal statement says a closed subspace of a normal space is normal. The Lean statement takes a topological space X with `NormalSpace X`, a subset A with `IsClosed A`, and concludes `NormalSpace {x // x ∈ A}`. The subtype `{x // x ∈ A}` automatically carries the subspace topology in Mathlib, so `NormalSpace` on this subtype exactly means the closed subspace A (with the subspace topology) is normal. The hypotheses (normal ambient space, closed subset) and conclusion (subspace is normal) match the informal claim precisely, with no added or omitted assumptions.

The informal statement says: if the product ∏ X_α is regular (assuming each X_α is nonempty), then each X_α is regular. The Lean signature takes an index type ι, a family of topological spaces X i, the hypothesis that each X i is Nonempty, and the hypothesis that the product Π i, X i is a RegularSpace, then concludes ∀ i, RegularSpace (X i). All of the hypotheses and the conclusion correspond directly: the nonempty assumption, the regularity of the product, and the regularity of each factor. The universal quantification over the index i is also preserved. This is a faithful formalization.

The Lean signature takes a topological space `X` with `LocallyCompactSpace X` and `T2Space X` hypotheses and concludes `RegularSpace X`, matching the informal claim "every locally compact Hausdorff space is regular". Mathlib's `RegularSpace` captures the point-closed-set separation condition; combined with the given `T2Space` hypothesis, it yields Munkres's notion of regular (which includes T1, automatically provided by T2). Mathlib's `LocallyCompactSpace` (every neighborhood contains a compact neighborhood) coincides with the usual "compact neighborhood" definition under the Hausdorff hypothesis. The quantifier structure (universal over topological spaces X) is correctly encoded via the implicit `{X : Type*}` and typeclass hypotheses. No extra or missing hypotheses, and no drift in conclusion.

The Lean statement captures the same claim: in a completely regular space, disjoint closed sets A (compact) and B can be separated by a continuous [0,1]-valued function sending A to 0 and B to 1. The target type `X → I` (with `I = Icc 0 1`) correctly encodes `f : X → [0,1]`, and `f '' A = {0}` corresponds to the informal image equality `f(A) = {0}`. The added `A.Nonempty` and `B.Nonempty` hypotheses make explicit what is already required by the informal conclusion: `f(A) = {0}` as image equality forces A to be nonempty, so the informal statement implicitly presupposes this. Under either the strict reading (where empty A would falsify the conclusion) or the loose reading (where empty A makes things vacuous), the quantified statements are logically equivalent. No hypothesis drift, no type mismatch, no weakening or strengthening of the mathematical claim.

In Munkres (the source of this exercise), "completely regular" includes the T1 axiom (one-point sets are closed), i.e. it means Tychonoff / T₃.₅. Mathlib's `CompletelyRegularSpace`, however, is defined as only the separation property (continuous f to [0,1] separating a point from a closed set) and does NOT require T0/T1 — T₃.₅ is captured by `T35Space`, which combines `CompletelyRegularSpace` with `T0Space`. The Lean signature therefore drops the T1 hypothesis that Munkres builds into "completely regular," so it claims the biconditional for a strictly larger class of spaces than Munkres asserts. The conclusion `IsConnected (univ : Set X) ↔ IsConnected (univ : Set (StoneCech X))` otherwise faithfully renders "X is connected iff βX is connected," but because the hypothesis is weaker, the Lean statement logically implies (a superset of) the informal claim while the informal claim does not imply the Lean one — so Lean is strictly stronger.

The Lean signature captures the informal claim exactly: given an injective f : ℕ → ℕ and a sequence p converging to a, the rearranged sequence p ∘ f also converges to a. The quantifier structure is correct — the informal statement asks for the one-direction implication (which is the only sensible reading when f is merely injective, not bijective, making q essentially a subsequence). The hypothesis `Injective f` matches "f is an injection", and `Tendsto (fun n => p (f n)) atTop (𝓝 a)` correctly encodes "q_k = p_{f(k)} converges to the same limit". The generalization from Pugh's real-analysis context to an arbitrary topological space is a natural abstraction that doesn't change the mathematical content of the claim — the statement and its proof go through verbatim.

The Lean statement sets O to the set of open subsets and C to the set of closed subsets of the metric space M, then asserts the existence of a bijective function f : O → C. Using Set-as-subtype coercion, O → C is a function from the subtype of open sets to the subtype of closed sets, and Bijective f asserts injectivity and surjectivity. This matches the informal claim of a bijection between the collection of open subsets and the collection of closed subsets. No extraneous hypotheses are added and no hypotheses of the informal statement are dropped.

The Lean theorem universally quantifies over all finite-dimensional real normed spaces E of dimension m and asserts the closed unit ball is compact. This is logically equivalent to the informal claim "for any norm on ℝ^m, the closed unit ball is compact": every m-dimensional real normed space is linearly isometric to ℝ^m equipped with some norm (by transporting the norm along any linear isomorphism), so quantifying over all such E is the same as quantifying over all norms on ℝ^m. The Metric.closedBall (0 : E) 1 precisely encodes {x : ‖x‖ ≤ 1}, and the finrank = m hypothesis pins the dimension. No extra hypotheses are smuggled in and nothing essential is dropped.

The informal statement asks to show that "S connected" does not in general imply "interior S connected" — a counterexample claim. The Lean signature formalizes this as ∃ S : Set (ℝ × ℝ), IsConnected S ∧ ¬ IsConnected (interior S), which is exactly the existential counterexample. The choice of ambient space ℝ × ℝ is appropriate given this is Pugh Exercise 2.57, set in ℝⁿ, and the standard counterexample (e.g., two tangent closed disks) lives in ℝ². IsConnected's nonempty+preconnected definition matches the standard reading of "connected" in Pugh. Producing a counterexample in ℝ² suffices to refute the general implication, so the formalization captures the intended meaning precisely.

The informal statement asserts that every uncountable subset E ⊆ ℝ has a condensation point — a point p such that every neighborhood of p meets E in uncountably many points. The Lean hypothesis `¬ Set.Countable E` matches "E uncountable", and the conclusion `∃ p : ℝ, ∀ U ∈ 𝓝 p, ¬ Set.Countable (U ∩ E)` is precisely the standard definition of "p is a condensation point of E" (every neighborhood of p intersects E in an uncountable set). The quantifier structure (∃ p, ∀ neighborhoods U) matches, p is not required to lie in E (as in the informal statement), and the ambient space is ℝ in both. No hidden assumptions are added or removed.

The informal statement asserts that √(n+1) − √n → 0 as n → ∞ over natural numbers. The Lean theorem uses `Tendsto (λ n => sqrt (n+1) - sqrt n) atTop (𝓝 0)`. The bound `n` in the lambda shadows the outer `(n : ℕ)` parameter; Lean will elaborate its type, and with `Real.sqrt : ℝ → ℝ` plus the default numeric choice, this is naturally `ℕ` (giving the standard sequence limit matching the informal claim). The outer unused `(n : ℕ)` parameter is harmless — since `ℕ` is inhabited, a universal quantification over an unused variable is logically equivalent to the conclusion alone. Thus the Lean statement expresses the same mathematical claim as the informal one.

The Lean statement asserts `¬ Summable f` for `f k = 1/(k * (Real.log k)^p)` when `p ≤ 1`, which matches the informal claim that the Bertrand series diverges for `p ≤ 1`. The potentially problematic edge cases at k = 0, 1 (where `Real.log k = 0`) produce only finitely many bounded terms (0 or 1 depending on p via `Real.rpow` conventions), which cannot affect whether the infinite series converges. For k ≥ 2 the terms are positive, so `¬ Summable` is equivalent to "diverges" in the standard sense. The universal quantification over p is correctly represented, and all real `p ≤ 1` (including p = 0 giving the harmonic series and p < 0) correctly yield a divergent series.

The informal statement requires f : ℝ → ℝ with continuous third derivative, and asks for existence of a point a where f(a)·f'(a)·f''(a)·f'''(a) ≥ 0. The Lean statement uses `ContDiff ℝ 3 f`, which in Mathlib means f has continuous derivatives up to order 3 — matching "continuous third derivative". The product uses `f a` (= 0th derivative), `deriv f a` (first derivative, equivalent to iteratedDeriv 1), `iteratedDeriv 2 f a` (second derivative), and `iteratedDeriv 3 f a` (third derivative), exactly matching f · f' · f'' · f'''. The existence quantifier and the ≥ 0 conclusion match directly. No extra or missing hypotheses.

The "infinitely many" encoding as "∀ N : ℕ, ∃ n : ℕ, n > N ∧ P n" is the standard Lean idiom equivalent to an infinite set of witnesses. Using ℕ for n rather than ℤ is harmless: any integer n that is a sum of two integer squares is necessarily non-negative, so the integer and natural formulations describe the same set. Similarly, for m ∈ ℕ, m = a² + b² has a solution in ℤ iff it has one in ℕ (since (-a)² = a²), so using i : Fin 6 → ℕ for the squares is equivalent to using integers. The three conditions n, n+1, n+2 being sums of two squares are each correctly captured. Thus the Lean statement is logically equivalent to the informal one.

The informal statement asks to show that for each positive integer n, the number $10^{10^{10^n}}+10^{10^n}+10^n-1$ is not prime. In Lean, `^` is right-associative, so `10^10^10^n` parses as `10^(10^(10^n))` and `10^10^n` as `10^(10^n)`, matching the informal expression exactly. The hypothesis `n > 0` corresponds to "positive integer n". Since the number is clearly positive, natural-number subtraction by 1 coincides with integer subtraction. `¬ Nat.Prime (...)` correctly formalizes "is not prime". No hypotheses or conclusions are added or dropped.

The Lean signature correctly captures the informal claim. The coefficient constraint `c n = 0 ∨ c n = 1` matches "each c_i is 0 or 1". The definition `f = λ x => ∑' i, c i * x^i` via `tsum` agrees with the power series sum at |x| < 1, and since both 2/3 and 1/2 lie within the radius of convergence (coefficients bounded by 1), the `tsum` yields the actual mathematical sum at these points. The hypothesis f(2/3)=3/2 and conclusion Irrational(f(1/2)) directly mirror the informal statement. The implicit universal quantification over f and c gives the desired "for all such power series" reading.

The informal statement defines $f_n(z) = n + (n-1)z + (n-2)z^2 + \cdots + z^{n-1} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}(n-i)z^i$ and claims no roots in $\{z : |z| \leq 1\}$. The Lean definition `∑ i : Fin n, (n-i) * z^(i : ℕ)` sums over $i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}$ with coefficient $(n-i)$, giving exactly $n + (n-1)z + \cdots + 1 \cdot z^{n-1}$. Since $i < n$ for $i : \text{Fin } n$, natural subtraction $(n-i)$ gives the correct value before coercion to $\mathbb{C}$. The hypothesis `hn : n > 0` matches "positive integer", and the conclusion `¬ (∃ z : ℂ, ‖z‖ ≤ 1 ∧ f n z = 0)` using the standard complex norm $\|z\| = |z|$ correctly captures "no roots in the closed unit disk". The universal `∀ n` in `hf` is just a convenient way to define the family for all natural numbers; the claim still applies only to the given $n > 0$.

The Lean formalization encodes the four complex numbers as a function `z : Fin 4 → ℂ`, with the only cosmetic change being reindexing from {1,2,3,4} to {0,1,2,3}. The hypothesis `‖z n‖ = 1` is exactly the unit modulus condition |z_j| = 1 in ℂ, and `z n ≠ 1` matches z_j ≠ 1. The conclusion `3 - z 0 - z 1 - z 2 - z 3 + (z 0)*(z 1)*(z 2)*(z 3) ≠ 0` is identical to the informal target expression. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and no types are altered.

The Lean statement captures exactly the informal claim: it universally quantifies over a real x and a rational y, assumes y ≠ 0 (matching the informal "r ≠ 0"), assumes x is irrational, and concludes that x + y (with y coerced to ℝ) is irrational, which is the same as r + x being irrational by commutativity. The types (ℝ for x, ℚ for the rational) and the quantifier structure align with the informal reading. The nonzero hypothesis, though mathematically redundant, appears in both versions, so no hypothesis has been silently added or dropped.

The informal statement asks to prove that no rational number has square equal to 12. The Lean signature states ¬ ∃ (x : ℚ), x ^ 2 = 12, which is precisely the negation of the existence of a rational x with x² = 12. The type ℚ correctly represents the rationals, the quantifier structure (negated existential) matches, and the conclusion x^2 = 12 matches "whose square is 12". No hypotheses are added or omitted. This is a faithful formalization.

The Lean hypotheses match the informal ones: A is nonempty (hA) and bounded below (hA_bdd_below). The set minus_A is defined as {x | -x ∈ A}, which is exactly the set {-x : x ∈ A} (y is in this set iff -y ∈ A iff y = -x for some x ∈ A). The conclusion sInf A = - sSup minus_A matches inf A = -sup(-A). Since A is nonempty and bounded below, -A is nonempty and bounded above, so sInf and sSup yield the genuine infimum and supremum. The formalization is logically equivalent to the informal claim.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement exactly: quantifies over all z : ℂ, asserts existence of a real r ≥ 0 and a complex w with ‖w‖ = 1 (equivalent to |w| = 1 for complex numbers in Mathlib) satisfying z = r * w. The multiplication r * w between ℝ and ℂ works via coercion and captures rw from the informal statement. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the conclusion is neither weakened nor strengthened. The z = 0 case is naturally covered by choosing r = 0 with any unit w.

The informal statement is the reverse triangle inequality for complex numbers: ||x|-|y|| ≤ |x-y|. In Lean, `‖x‖` denotes the norm of a complex number, which coincides with the complex absolute value |x|. The outer `|·|` is applied to `‖x‖ - ‖y‖`, which is a real number, so it denotes the real absolute value. Thus `|‖x‖ - ‖y‖| ≤ ‖x-y‖` matches the informal claim exactly, with universally quantified x, y : ℂ. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the types align.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement: n ≥ 3, x, y in EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n), ‖x - y‖ = d, d > 0, r > 0, 2r > d, and the conclusion is that the set {z | ‖z - x‖ = r ∧ ‖z - y‖ = r} is infinite. The unused parameter `_z : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` is vestigial — since EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) is always inhabited (has 0), requiring an arbitrary unused witness of this type does not add any nontrivial assumption or change the logical content. All hypotheses, types, and the conclusion correspond exactly to the informal claim.

The informal statement asks: for k ≥ 2 and x ∈ R^k, there exists y ∈ R^k with y ≠ 0 and x·y = 0. The Lean version uses n > 1 (equivalent to n ≥ 2 for naturals), uses EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) which is the standard formalization of R^n, asserts ∃ y with y ≠ 0, and uses the real inner product ⟪x, y⟫_ℝ = 0, which matches the dot product on EuclideanSpace. Quantifier structure (∀ x, ∃ y) is preserved via x being a parameter and y existentially quantified. No extra hypotheses or weakened conclusions are introduced.

The informal statement says: if every infinite subset of a metric space X has a limit point, then X is separable. The Lean signature requires X to be a MetricSpace, hypothesizes that every infinite subset A has some p : X with `AccPt p (𝓟 A)`, and concludes `SeparableSpace X`. In Mathlib, `AccPt p (𝓟 A)` unfolds to `NeBot (𝓝[≠] p ⊓ 𝓟 A)`, meaning every punctured neighborhood of p meets A — exactly Rudin's definition of a limit point of A (the point need not lie in A, matching the informal reading). `SeparableSpace X` is the standard "has a countable dense subset," matching "separable." Quantifier structure and types all align, so this is a faithful formalization.

The Lean signature faithfully encodes the informal claim. The ambient space `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)` is the standard formalization of $\mathbb{R}^k$; `¬ Set.Countable E` is "E is uncountable"; the defining predicate `∀ U ∈ 𝓝 x, ¬ Set.Countable (U ∩ E)` is exactly the standard definition of a condensation point; and `Perfect P` in Mathlib is defined as closed with no isolated points, which is the standard notion of a perfect set. Binding both E and P as parameters with the hypothesis `hP` pinning P to the condensation set is equivalent to "let P be the set of condensation points of E". No hypotheses are silently added or dropped.

The informal statement asserts that every closed set A in a separable metric space decomposes as A = P₁ ∪ P₂ where P₁ is perfect (possibly empty) and P₂ is at most countable. The Lean signature captures exactly this: it quantifies over a separable metric space `X`, takes a closed set `A`, and asserts the existence of `P₁, P₂` with `A = P₁ ∪ P₂`, `Perfect P₁`, and `Set.Countable P₂`. Mathlib's `Perfect` definition does not require nonemptiness (the empty set vacuously satisfies `∀ x ∈ C, AccPt x (𝓟 C)`), so the "possibly empty" clause is correctly represented. `Set.Countable` in Mathlib means "at most countable" (i.e., finite or countably infinite), matching the informal "at most countable." The decomposition is stated as a union (not disjoint union), matching the informal phrasing.

The informal statement (Rudin Exercise 3.1a) concerns sequences of complex numbers, and the Lean formalization uses `f : ℕ → ℂ` accordingly. The hypothesis `∃ a : ℂ, Tendsto f atTop (𝓝 a)` precisely expresses "$\{s_n\}$ converges," and the conclusion `∃ a : ℝ, Tendsto (‖f ·‖) atTop (𝓝 a)` precisely expresses "$\{|s_n|\}$ converges," since `‖·‖` on ℂ agrees with the usual modulus $|·|$ and the limit of moduli is real. No extra hypotheses (e.g., boundedness, decidability) are imposed, and no conclusion is weakened or strengthened. The quantifier structure and types match the standard reading of the informal claim.

The Lean definition `f 0 = √2` and `f (n+1) = √(2 + √(f n))` matches the informal recursion $s_1 = \sqrt{2}$, $s_{n+1} = \sqrt{2+\sqrt{s_n}}$ up to a harmless index shift (Lean's `f n` = informal $s_{n+1}$). The theorem asserts both required claims: existence of a limit (`∃ x, Tendsto f atTop (𝓝 x)`) which is exactly the convergence of the sequence, and the bound `∀ n, f n < 2`, which under the index shift covers all informal indices $n \ge 1$. The conjunction, quantifier structure, and types (ℕ → ℝ) all align with the informal claim, so the Lean signature is logically equivalent.

The Lean signature defines `g n = Real.sqrt (n+1) - Real.sqrt n`, which matches $a_i = \sqrt{i+1} - \sqrt{i}$. The summation `∑ i ∈ range n, g i` corresponds exactly to $\sum_{i < n} a_i$. The claim `Tendsto (fun n => ...) atTop atTop` is the standard Mathlib encoding of $\lim_{n \to \infty} (\ldots) = \infty$ for a real-valued sequence. All types (`ℕ` index, `ℝ` values) and quantifier structure match the informal statement.

The Lean statement captures each piece of the informal claim accurately: `h1` states that the partial sums of $a_n$ converge (matching "$\Sigma a_n$ converges"); `h2` encodes "monotonic" as `Monotone b ∨ Antitone b`, which is the standard meaning in Rudin (either non-decreasing or non-increasing); `h3` uses `Bornology.IsBounded (Set.range b)`, which is the standard way to say a real sequence is bounded; and the conclusion asserts convergence of the partial sums of $a_n b_n$. The types (`ℕ → ℝ`) and the quantifier structure (existence of a limit for the partial sums) align with the informal statement. No extraneous hypotheses are added, and none are dropped.

The Lean statement captures the informal claim precisely. The metric space `X` is arbitrary, `p : ℕ → X` is the sequence, and `φ : ℕ → ℕ` with `StrictMono φ` correctly encodes the notion of a subsequence via `p ∘ φ`. The hypothesis `CauchySeq p` matches "Cauchy sequence", `Tendsto (p ∘ φ) atTop (𝓝 r)` matches "subsequence converges to p", and the conclusion `Tendsto p atTop (𝓝 r)` matches "the full sequence converges to p". No extra hypotheses (like completeness, nonemptyness, etc.) are added, and none are dropped.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement: `X` is typed as a `MetricSpace` with `CompleteSpace` and `Nonempty` instances (nonempty complete metric space), `G : ℕ → Set X` encodes the sequence, and `hG` requires each `G n` to be both open and dense. The conclusion `∃ x, ∀ n, x ∈ G n` is logically equivalent to `⋂ n, G n ≠ ∅`, which is exactly Baire's theorem's claim. The indexing starting at 0 versus 1 in ℕ is an inconsequential reindexing of the same sequence of open dense sets, so the intersection is mathematically identical. No hypotheses are added or dropped.

The Lean statement matches the informal claim. Both α and β are metric spaces, f is a function between them with the continuity hypothesis, and the conclusion ∀ (x : Set α), f '' (closure x) ⊆ closure (f '' x) directly encodes "f(closure E) ⊂ closure(f(E)) for every set E ⊂ X". The universal quantifier over subsets, the image operation `f ''`, the `closure` operation, and the subset relation all align exactly with the informal statement. No extra hypotheses (e.g., completeness, compactness) are imposed, and none are silently dropped.

The Lean statement `f '' Set.univ ⊆ closure (f '' s)` asserts $f(X) \subseteq \overline{f(E)}$, which is exactly the definition of "$f(E)$ is dense in $f(X)$" (since $f(E) \subseteq f(X)$, density in the subspace topology on $f(X)$ is equivalent to every point of $f(X)$ lying in the closure of $f(E)$ in $Y$). The hypotheses match: both spaces are metric spaces, $f$ is continuous, and $s$ is dense in $\alpha$. The extraneous mention of "$g$" in the informal statement is an artifact of the original Rudin exercise (part (a) only concerns $f$), so its omission is correct.

This is Tietze's extension theorem (Rudin Exercise 4.5a) for ℝ. The Lean statement captures the same claim: given a closed set E ⊂ ℝ and a function continuous on E (encoded as f : ℝ → ℝ with ContinuousOn f E, which is the standard Lean/Mathlib idiom for a function "defined on E" since values outside E are irrelevant), there exists a globally continuous g : ℝ → ℝ that agrees with f on E. The hypotheses (E closed, f continuous on E), the existential conclusion, and the equality condition restricted to E all match. The ℝ → ℝ encoding is equivalent to a function on E since any extension outside E preserves ContinuousOn, and conversely any function on E can be extended arbitrarily to ℝ. No extra assumptions are added and none are dropped.

The Lean theorem captures the informal claim precisely: E is hypothesized to be compact (h₁), the graph G is defined as {(x, f x) | x ∈ E} (h₂), and the conclusion is the biconditional ContinuousOn f E ↔ IsCompact G. Although f is typed as ℝ → ℝ (total) rather than a partial function on E, this is immaterial because ContinuousOn f E only uses f's values on E, and the graph definition also only uses values on E — so the statement is mathematically identical to the informal "f defined on E" reading. All relevant quantifiers, types, and the iff structure match.

The informal statement is literally existential: "there exists a real function f such that f is uniformly continuous and is bounded on E" — i.e. ∃ f, UC(f) ∧ f bounded on E. This is trivially true (take f ≡ 0). The Lean statement is universal: ∀ f, UniformContinuousOn f E → f(E) is bounded. The Lean universal implies the informal existential (the constant 0 is a witness), but the informal existential does not imply the Lean universal. So the Lean encodes strictly more than the literal informal claim. (It is clearly intended to be Rudin 4.8(a), whose correct form is the universal in the Lean; the informal statement appears garbled, but judged literally, the Lean is stronger.)

The Lean signature states exactly that the composition of two uniformly continuous functions between uniform spaces is uniformly continuous, matching the informal claim. The hypotheses `UniformContinuous f` and `UniformContinuous g` and the conclusion `UniformContinuous (g ∘ f)` correspond directly to "a uniformly continuous function of a uniformly continuous function is uniformly continuous." Although Rudin's original context is metric spaces, uniform continuity is naturally defined on uniform spaces (which include metric spaces as instances), and the claim holds verbatim in this setting without adding or dropping any mathematical content. Quantifier structure over the spaces and functions matches the implicit universal quantification in the informal statement.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement. The IVP hypothesis `hf` requires a < b and considers both `f a < c < f b` and `f b < c < f a`; this is logically equivalent to the informal "if f(a) < c < f(b), then f(x)=c for some x between a and b", where a and b are not ordered (the informal version handles b < a by symmetry of "between"). The strict a < x < b is fine since f(a) ≠ c ≠ f(b) forces x to be strictly inside. The `hg` hypothesis quantifies over ℚ and asserts closedness of {x | f x = r}, matching "for every rational r, the set of all x with f(x)=r is closed." The conclusion Continuous f matches exactly. No hidden hypotheses are added or removed.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement. `ContinuousOn f (Set.Ioo a b)` corresponds to "f continuous on (a,b)", the hypothesis `h` encodes the midpoint convexity condition exactly for all x, y ∈ (a,b), and `ConvexOn ℝ (Set.Ioo a b) f` is Mathlib's standard definition of convexity of f on the open interval, which matches "f is convex" in the usual analytic sense. The extra `hab : a < b` is implicit in the informal notation "(a,b)" as a nontrivial open interval and does not meaningfully strengthen or weaken the statement, since the a ≥ b case makes both sides trivially true.

The Lean statement captures the three conclusions: strict monotonicity of f on (a,b), differentiability of g on the image f((a,b)), and the inverse derivative formula g'(f(x)) = 1/f'(x) for x ∈ (a,b). The hypothesis `deriv f x > 0` implicitly encodes differentiability (since Lean's `deriv` is 0 at non-differentiable points, positivity forces differentiability), matching the informal "f'(x) > 0 in (a,b)". The hypothesis `g(f x) = x` on (a,b) gives g as a left inverse; while weaker than "g is THE inverse function" globally, f is injective on (a,b) (after proving strict monotonicity), so g is uniquely determined on f((a,b)), which is an open interval — and since DifferentiableOn on an open set plus `deriv g` at points in that open set only depend on g's values on f((a,b)), the conclusions are equivalent to those of the informal statement. The universal quantification over a,b (including degenerate cases a ≥ b) is vacuously fine. Thus the Lean formalization is logically equivalent to the informal claim.

The Lean signature encodes the hypothesis $\sum_{i=0}^{n} C_i/(i+1) = 0$ as `∑ i ∈ range (n + 1), (C i) / (i + 1) = 0`, which correctly ranges i over 0, 1, ..., n giving denominators 1, 2, ..., n+1 — matching the informal sum $C_0 + C_1/2 + \cdots + C_n/(n+1)$. The conclusion `∃ x ∈ Set.Ioo 0 1, ∑ i ∈ range (n + 1), (C i) * (x^i) = 0` correctly captures "at least one real root strictly between 0 and 1" via the open interval. Typing `C : ℕ → ℝ` is a harmless encoding (only values at 0..n are used) and matches "real constants". The coercions of `i + 1` to ℝ under division are standard and semantically correct. No hypotheses are dropped or added, and no notions are strengthened or weakened.

Each informal hypothesis maps precisely to the Lean version: continuity on [0,∞) via ContinuousOn on Set.Ici 0; differentiability on (0,∞) via DifferentiableOn on Set.Ioi 0; f(0)=0 directly; and f' monotone increasing via MonotoneOn (deriv f) on Set.Ioi 0. Since f is differentiable on Set.Ioi 0, `deriv f` agrees with the actual derivative there, so using `deriv f` is safe. Rudin defines "monotonically increasing" as non-decreasing (Def. 4.28), matching Mathlib's MonotoneOn. The conclusion restricts g = f x / x to Set.Ioi 0, matching the informal "g is monotonically increasing for x > 0". No hidden hypotheses are added or dropped.

The Lean signature faithfully captures the informal statement. Twice-differentiability on (a,∞) is encoded via DifferentiableOn for both f and deriv f. The least upper bounds M0, M1, M2 are defined via sSup, with explicit boundedness hypotheses (hbdd0/1/2) ensuring sSup equals the actual least upper bound (needed because Mathlib's sSup returns 0 for unbounded sets); this matches the informal claim that these suprema exist as real numbers. The conclusion M1² ≤ 4·M0·M2 matches exactly. Since (a,∞) is open, `deriv f` agrees with f' on (a,∞), so the encoding is logically equivalent.
